title: Time Series & Forecasting in Practice
pitchHtml: Forecasting is one of the most sellable capabilities a pole can offer — demand, sales, cash, capacity, headcount, energy load — and one of the easiest to get embarrassingly wrong, because time breaks the usual ML assumptions. Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice introduces forecasting as one tool among many; this module is its dedicated treatment: what makes time series special, the classical → ML → deep-learning ladder, how to evaluate a forecaster honestly (the part teams fool themselves on), and how to scope a forecasting mission so you sell a decision-grade range, not a fragile point estimate. The value-vs-complexity lesson is sharper here than almost anywhere: simple methods and ensembles routinely beat deep learning on real series, and the discipline that matters most — respecting time order in validation — costs nothing but attention.

items.html: Explain what makes time-series data special (temporal order, autocorrelation, trend, seasonality, stationarity) and why it forbids a random train/test split
Route a forecasting problem across classical (ETS/ARIMA) → ML (boosted trees on lag features) → deep / foundation models, and justify not defaulting to deep learning
Insist on backtesting / walk-forward validation and read the right error metric (MAPE, sMAPE, MASE) for the situation
Demand a seasonal-naïve baseline that every forecast must beat, and recognise the M-competition lesson in a roadmap
Decide when 2026 time-series foundation models are worth it versus a classical baseline
Frame forecasts as probabilistic ranges for decisions, and set horizon + retraining cadence sensibly
Scope and price a demand, capacity, inventory, or energy-forecasting mission with a named deliverable and a buyer

blocks.html:
A time series is data indexed by time, where the order is information. That single fact breaks the assumption underneath most ML — that rows are independent and interchangeable. You cannot shuffle, you cannot peek forward, and "accuracy on past data" can be a mirage if you let the future leak into the past. Five properties define the territory:

  • Temporal order — row ttt comes after row t−1t-1t−1; rearranging them destroys the signal.
  • Autocorrelation — a value is correlated with its own recent past (today's sales resemble yesterday's). This is the thing models exploit, and the thing that invalidates naïve statistics.
  • Trend — the long-run direction (revenue drifting up 4%/year).
  • Seasonality — patterns that repeat on a fixed, known period (weekly staffing dips, December retail peaks).
  • Cycles — longer swings with no fixed period (business cycles, multi-year commodity waves) — distinct from seasonality precisely because you can't set your watch by them.

Decomposition. Almost any series can be split into structural parts plus what's left over:
yt=trendt+seasonalt+residualty_t = \text{trend}_t + \text{seasonal}_t + \text{residual}_tyt​=trendt​+seasonalt​+residualt​

where yty_tyt​ is the observed value at time ttt, trendt\text{trend}_ttrendt​ is the slow underlying direction, seasonalt\text{seasonal}_tseasonalt​ is the repeating fixed-period pattern, and residualt\text{residual}_tresidualt​ is the irreducible noise you should not try to fit. This is the additive form. When the seasonal swings grow with the level of the series (holiday spikes that get bigger every year as the business grows), use the multiplicative form yt=trendt×seasonalt×residualty_t = \text{trend}_t \times \text{seasonal}_t \times \text{residual}_tyt​=trendt​×seasonalt​×residualt​ — or just model log⁡yt\log y_tlogyt​, which turns multiplicative structure back into additive.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Decomposition is the mental model behind nearly every classical method: estimate the trend, estimate the repeating seasonal shape, and what remains should look like random noise. If the "residual" still has visible pattern, your model is leaving money on the table. Additive vs multiplicative is just one question — do the seasonal swings stay the same size, or grow with the business?

Stationarity and differencing. Many classical methods assume the series is stationary — its statistical behaviour (mean, variance, autocorrelation) doesn't change over time. Real business series rarely are: they trend and they breathe. The standard fix is differencing — model the change from one period to the next rather than the level itself:
yt′=yt−yt−1y'_t = y_t - y_{t-1}yt′​=yt​−yt−1​

where yt′y'_tyt′​ is the differenced series. A trending series ("sales keep rising") often becomes roughly flat-and-stationary once you look at month-over-month change instead of the raw level. Seasonal differencing (yt−yt−my_t - y_{t-m}yt​−yt−m​ for period mmm) removes a repeating pattern the same way.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Differencing is "stop modelling where you are, model how fast you're moving." A staircase climbing steadily looks non-stationary; the step size between stairs is constant and stationary. That step size is what stationary models can actually predict. The "I" in ARIMA is exactly this differencing step.

subsections.title: Autocorrelation (ACF / PACF): How Much Does the Past Predict the Present?

blocks.html:
Before picking a model, you ask: how far back does the past still influence today? Two diagnostic plots answer it:

  • ACF (autocorrelation function) — correlation between the series and itself at each lag (1 period ago, 2 periods ago…). A slow decay signals trend; a spike every 7 or 12 lags signals weekly or monthly seasonality.
  • PACF (partial autocorrelation function) — the correlation at each lag after removing the influence of the shorter lags, isolating the "direct" effect of lag kkk.

For a tech lead, you don't read these plots yourself — but you should know they exist, because they are how a practitioner chooses which lags to feed a model (the ppp and qqq in ARIMA, or which lag features to engineer for a boosted tree). If someone proposes lag features with no look at autocorrelation, they're guessing.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: ACF answers "does last week predict this week?" PACF answers "does last week still add anything once I already know yesterday?" Together they tell you how long the memory of the series is — and a series with strong autocorrelation at lag 12 is telling you it's annual, before any model is fit.

subsections.title: Classical Methods: Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, ARIMA

blocks.html: The humbling, repeatedly-confirmed truth (see §7, the M-competition lesson) is that these cheap, transparent methods win or tie on most real business series. Start here.

MethodOne-line intuitionGood for
Naïve / seasonal-naïve"Tomorrow ≈ today" / "this December ≈ last December"The baseline every model must beat
Moving averageAverage the last kkk points to smooth out noiseStable, slow-moving series; quick smoothing
Exponential smoothing (ETS / Holt-Winters)Weighted average that forgets the past gradually; extends to trend + seasonalityThe robust go-to for most single business series
ARIMA(p,d,q)Predict the next value from its own recent values and recent errorsStationary-ish series; classic and well-understood

Moving average smooths by averaging a sliding window of the last kkk observations — it kills noise but lags behind turning points and can't extrapolate trend on its own.

Exponential smoothing predicts the next level as a weighted blend of the newest observation and the previous smoothed estimate:
st=α xt+(1−α) st−1s_t = \alpha\, x_t + (1 - \alpha)\, s_{t-1}st​=αxt​+(1−α)st−1​

where sts_tst​ is the smoothed value at time ttt, xtx_txt​ is the newest actual observation, st−1s_{t-1}st−1​ is the previous smoothed value, and α∈[0,1]\alpha \in [0,1]α∈[0,1] is the smoothing factor that sets how fast the model forgets the past. The full Holt-Winters family extends this with one equation each for trend and for seasonality, which is why ETS handles most business series out of the box. Use case: a robust, near-zero-cost forecaster for a single series with trend and/or seasonality.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: α\alphaα is a memory dial. High α\alphaα (near 1) reacts fast to the latest point but stays jumpy; low α\alphaα (near 0) is smooth and slow, leaning on history. Every past point keeps a weight that decays geometrically — recent data matters most, older data fades rather than vanishing — which is exactly what "exponential" names.

ARIMA(p, d, q) combines three ideas, and the three letters are the recipe:

  • AR (auto-regressive), order ppp — predict the next value from a weighted sum of the last ppp values: yt=c+ϕ1yt−1+⋯+ϕpyt−p+εty_t = c + \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \dots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_tyt​=c+ϕ1​yt−1​+⋯+ϕp​yt−p​+εt​. "The future is a function of the recent past."
  • I (integrated), order ddd — difference the series ddd times first (§1) to make it stationary. "Model the change, not the level."
  • MA (moving-average), order qqq — also correct using the last qqq forecast errors εt−1…εt−q\varepsilon_{t-1}\dots\varepsilon_{t-q}εt−1​…εt−q​. "Learn from how wrong I just was."

You don't hand-tune (p,d,q)(p, d, q)(p,d,q) — auto-ARIMA searches them, guided by the ACF/PACF intuition of §2. SARIMA adds a seasonal copy of the same three orders for series with a fixed period.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: ARIMA says the next value is explained by three things: where the recent values were (AR), the fact that we're modelling change rather than level (I), and the pattern in our own recent mistakes (MA). It's powerful and transparent on a single well-behaved series, but it struggles to absorb external drivers (price, weather, promotions) — which is exactly where ML takes over.

When classical wins: one or a few series, clear and stable seasonality, limited history, a need for transparency, or you need a defensible answer this week. Don't reach past ETS/ARIMA until a baseline proves it leaves real money on the table.

subsections.title: ML for Time Series: Lag Features + Gradient-Boosted Trees

blocks.html:
The trick that unlocks the entire tabular ML toolbox for forecasting is feature engineering: turn "predict the next value of a series" into "predict a number from a row of features" by manufacturing the time structure as columns.

  • Lag features — the value 1, 7, 28, 365… periods ago (yesterday, last week, last year). This is how you hand autocorrelation to a model that has no notion of time.
  • Rolling-window features — rolling mean / min / max / std over the last kkk periods (a 7-day average captures recent level; a rolling std captures volatility).
  • Calendar features — day-of-week, month, holiday flags, "days until Black Friday", paydays. These encode seasonality and known events explicitly.
  • External drivers — price, promotions, weather, web traffic — the levers ARIMA can't easily absorb.

Feed those columns to gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost / LightGBM / CatBoost) and you very often have the practical winner: it captures interactions and non-linear effects, trains one model across all your series at once, ingests external drivers natively, and costs a fraction of a deep model. The mechanics of boosted trees live in Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice — here the point is that feature engineering, not the algorithm, is where the forecast is won or lost.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: A boosted tree has no idea what "time" is — it sees an unordered table. Lag and calendar features are the time series, re-expressed as columns it understands. Get those features right and a generic tabular model becomes a strong forecaster; get them wrong (or leak a future value into them) and no algorithm saves you. Critical discipline: every feature for time ttt must use only information available at ttt — a rolling mean that includes the target row is the classic leak.

subsections.title: Modern Methods: Deep Learning & Foundation Models

blocks.html:
When you genuinely have many related series and lots of history, deep learning earns a look. The headline architectures (worth recognising, not deriving — see Time Series Analysis & Forecasting in the AI Scientist Path):

  • DeepAR — trains one global recurrent model across thousands of related series (every SKU × store) and outputs probabilistic forecasts. Shines when individual series are short but you have many of them.
  • N-BEATS / N-HiTS — pure deep-learning forecasters (no recurrence) that are strong, fast, and competition-winning on univariate problems.
  • Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) — attention-based, handles many series plus static and time-varying covariates, and offers some interpretability of which inputs mattered.

2026 time-series foundation models — TimeGPT, Chronos, Moirai (in the Tools table) — are pre-trained on huge corpora of series and can produce a zero-shot forecast with no training on your data: point them at a history and they predict. They are genuinely useful for fast baselines, cold-start (little history), and breadth — but they are not an automatic win. On a single well-behaved series with clear seasonality, a tuned ETS or a boosted tree on good features still frequently matches or beats them, at a fraction of the cost and with full transparency.

SituationReach forWhy
One / few series, clear seasonalityETS / ARIMA / ProphetCheap, robust, transparent; hard to beat
Many series + external driversGradient-boosted trees on lag/calendar featuresCaptures interactions; one model, all series; cheap
Thousands of related series, long historyGlobal deep models (DeepAR / N-BEATS / TFT)Shares signal across series — but costly & complex
Need a forecast now, little/no historyFoundation model (zero-shot) or seasonal-naïveInstant; validate before trusting it

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The escalation mirrors the whole path: the more series and the richer the drivers, the more a complex model has to learn from — and the more its cost is justified. A single monthly sales line will never give a Transformer enough to beat "same as last December, adjusted for trend." Don't open with deep learning for "forecast our revenue."

A single number ("we'll sell 1,000 units") is almost always wrong and says nothing about how wrong. A range — "80% chance between 850 and 1,200" — is what lets a client actually decide: set safety stock, size a buffer, staff a shift, hedge a position. Two ways to produce one:

  • Prediction intervals — a band around the forecast (e.g. 80% or 95%) within which the actual is expected to land.
  • Quantile forecasts — predict specific percentiles directly (P10, P50, P90), which is exactly what asymmetric decisions need ("plan for the P90 demand so you don't stock out").

The decision usually isn't symmetric: the cost of under-forecasting (lost sales, stockout) rarely equals the cost of over-forecasting (holding cost, waste). A range lets the client pick the percentile that matches their cost trade-off — a point estimate hides that choice and quietly makes it for them.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: A point forecast answers "what's your single best guess?" A probabilistic forecast answers the question a planner actually has: "how bad could it get, and how often?" Selling a point as if it were certain is the fastest way to lose trust the first time reality lands outside it — insist on bands, and frame them in decision terms.

This is the section that separates a forecaster you can trust from one that flatters itself. It is also where most teams quietly cheat without meaning to.

Backtesting / walk-forward validation — non-negotiable. You cannot evaluate a forecaster with an ordinary random train/test split: shuffling lets the model train on the future and predict the past, and the score becomes fiction. Instead, train on the past, predict the next window, roll forward, repeat — exactly how the model is used in production.

blocks.text:
Fold 1:  train [────────]  test [──]
Fold 2:  train [──────────]  test [──]
Fold 3:  train [────────────]  test [──]
                          ... roll forward

blocks.html:
Each fold only ever trains on data that existed before the window it predicts. This single discipline is the most important thing in forecasting — and the most common place teams fool themselves. The broader validation toolkit lives in Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation; you must insist on it at scoping time.

The seasonal-naïve baseline every forecast must beat. Before any model is allowed to claim success, compare it to "this period = the same period one season ago" (this December = last December). A shocking number of "AI forecasting" projects fail to beat this one-line rule. If your model can't, you've either learned the problem is genuinely flat-predictable (ship the rule) or your model is broken. Either way it's the cheapest insight in the project.

Metrics — pick the one your client can reason in.

MAPE (mean absolute percentage error) — the one stakeholders quote, an average "% off":
MAPE=100%n∑i=1n∣yi−y^i∣∣yi∣\text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{|y_i - \hat{y}_i|}{|y_i|}MAPE=n100%​i=1∑n​∣yi​∣∣yi​−y^​i​∣​

with nnn the number of forecast points, yiy_iyi​ the actual, and y^i\hat{y}_iy^​i​ the forecast. Intuitive and scale-free, but it explodes when actuals are near zero and penalises over- and under-forecasting asymmetrically — unreliable for intermittent or low-volume series.

sMAPE (symmetric MAPE) patches the asymmetry by putting the average of actual and forecast in the denominator:
sMAPE=100%n∑i=1n∣yi−y^i∣(∣yi∣+∣y^i∣)/2\text{sMAPE} = \frac{100\%}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{|y_i - \hat{y}_i|}{(|y_i| + |\hat{y}_i|)/2}sMAPE=n100%​i=1∑n​(∣yi​∣+∣y^​i​∣)/2∣yi​−y^​i​∣​

It is bounded and treats over/under-shoot more evenly — but still misbehaves when values approach zero.

MASE (mean absolute scaled error) is the robust choice — it scales your error by the error of the seasonal-naïve baseline on the training data:
MASE=1n∑i=1n∣yi−y^i∣1T−m∑t=m+1T∣yt−yt−m∣\text{MASE} = \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|y_i - \hat{y}_i|}{\frac{1}{T-m}\sum_{t=m+1}^{T}|y_t - y_{t-m}|}MASE=T−m1​∑t=m+1T​∣yt​−yt−m​∣n1​∑i=1n​∣yi​−y^​i​∣​

where the numerator is your forecast's mean absolute error, and the denominator is the mean absolute error of the seasonal-naïve forecast (period mmm) over the TTT training points. MASE < 1 means you beat the naïve baseline; MASE > 1 means you lost to "same as last season."

blocks.title: why MASE is robust
blocks.html: It is scale-free without dividing by the actual value, so it never explodes near zero the way MAPE does, and it works across series of wildly different magnitudes. Best of all it bakes the baseline into the number: a single value tells you both how good the forecast is and whether it was worth building over the trivial rule. That's why competition organisers and serious forecasters favour it.

The M-competition lesson. The long-running M-competitions (large public forecasting bake-offs, Makridakis et al.) delivered a finding that should shape every forecasting roadmap: simple statistical methods, and especially combinations / ensembles of them, routinely match or beat complex methods on real series — and pure deep learning only pulls clearly ahead with many related series and abundant data. For a leader the takeaway is concrete: don't open with a Transformer for "forecast our monthly sales"; start with seasonal-naïve and ETS, try a simple average of a few methods, and escalate only on backtested evidence.

subsections.title: A Worked Example: Weekly Demand Forecast for Replenishment

blocks.html:
A retailer wants a weekly demand forecast per SKU per store to drive replenishment and reduce both stockouts and overstock.

  1. Frame the decision. The forecast feeds a reorder decision; the cost of under-forecasting (lost sale, empty shelf) outweighs over-forecasting (holding cost). So the deliverable must be a range, and planning will use a high percentile (P90), not the mean.
  2. Baseline first. Seasonal-naïve: "this week ≈ same week last year." Backtested with walk-forward folds, it gives the bar — say MASE = 1.0 by definition, MAPE ≈ 22%.
  3. Classical. ETS/Holt-Winters per series for the high-volume SKUs with clear seasonality. Beats naïve on the steady sellers (MASE ≈ 0.85); struggles on the long tail of intermittent, low-volume items (where MAPE is meaningless — judge those on MASE).
  4. ML escalation. One LightGBM model across all series, with lag features (1, 52 weeks), rolling means, calendar/holiday flags, price and promo columns. It captures the promo and price effects ETS can't, sharpens the whole catalogue (MASE ≈ 0.78), and is one model to maintain instead of thousands.
  5. Probabilistic output. Quantile regression (or LightGBM quantile objectives) to emit P10/P50/P90 per SKU-week, so the planner orders to the percentile that matches the item's cost trade-off.
  6. Deliver & operate. The deliverable is not "a model." It's a weekly forecasting service — ranges per SKU-store feeding the replenishment dashboard, backtested, with a monitoring + retraining cadence (§ horizon note below). Validation against held-out weeks, ideally an A/B of "forecast-driven vs old rule" reorder, is a Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation concern.

What carried the value: a clean baseline, the right features, one boosted-tree model, honest walk-forward validation, and ranges — not a frontier architecture.

Horizon & retraining cadence. Two scoping questions decide cost and credibility:

  • Horizon — how far ahead? Accuracy decays with distance; a one-week-ahead forecast is a different (and far easier) promise than twelve months out. Never promise precise long-horizon point forecasts — wider ranges are the honest answer as the horizon grows.
  • Retraining cadence — how often do you refit? Match it to how fast the world moves and how soon new actuals arrive: daily/weekly for fast retail demand, monthly or quarterly for slow strategic series. Bake the cadence (and drift monitoring) into the engagement, not as an afterthought.

Forecasting is among the most repeatable, fixed-scopable engagements a pole can sell. Four archetypes cover most demand.

  • Demand / Sales Forecasting — Problem: stock-outs and overstock, or blind revenue/cash planning. Deliverable: a weekly forecasting service producing per-SKU (or per-region) forecasts with uncertainty ranges, backtested against history, feeding a planning dashboard. Scope: 8-12 weeks, much of it data and feature work. Who buys: Supply Chain / Operations Director, Head of Planning, CFO (for cash & revenue).

  • Capacity & Workforce Planning — Problem: over- or under-staffing against fluctuating volume (call centres, field ops, warehouses, clinics). Deliverable: a volume forecast translated into a staffing/shift recommendation (forecast → required headcount per interval), with ranges so the client can choose a service-level buffer. Scope: 6-10 weeks. Who buys: COO, Head of Operations / WFM, contact-centre or clinic director.

  • Inventory / Supply-Chain Optimisation — Problem: working capital tied up in the wrong stock; service levels missed. Deliverable: probabilistic demand forecasts feeding reorder points and safety-stock levels (forecast P90 → safety stock per SKU-location), with a clear "order to this percentile" policy. Scope: 10-14 weeks (multi-echelon adds time). Who buys: Supply Chain Director, Head of Inventory / S&OP, CFO.

  • Energy or Load Forecasting — Problem: balancing generation, procurement, or grid load against weather-driven demand; costly imbalance penalties. Deliverable: a short-horizon load/consumption forecast (hourly/daily) with confidence bands, fed by weather and calendar drivers, into a trading or dispatch process. Scope: 8-12 weeks. Who buys: utility / energy-trading desk, grid operator, large industrial energy manager, BMS/facilities lead.

Buy-vs-build: the algorithms are commodity open-source (StatsForecast, LightGBM) or a cheap foundation-model API call — your billable value is the data work, the feature engineering, the honest backtesting, the probabilistic framing, and the wiring into the client's planning workflow. Don't sell "an ARIMA model"; sell the reorder/staffing/dispatch decision it powers, as a maintained service. What to charge for: the end-to-end build, plus a monitoring + retraining retainer (series drift; that recurring revenue is real and justified). What NOT to promise: a specific accuracy number before you've seen the data; precise long-horizon point forecasts (offer ranges, and be explicit that accuracy decays with horizon); "set and forget" — every forecaster decays. Commit to a method for finding and proving the number, never to the number.
As of 2026 — treat names/versions as perishable; re-check before quoting.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote
statsmodelsARIMA/SARIMA, ETS, classical statsOSSMature classical time-series toolbox
StatsForecast / NeuralForecast / HierarchicalForecast (Nixtla)Fast classical + modern forecasting at scaleOSSStrong for many-series; excellent baselines
Prophet (Meta)Analyst-friendly trend + seasonality + holidaysOSSFast to a decent daily forecast; not a silver bullet
LightGBM / XGBoost / CatBoostBoosted trees on lag/calendar featuresOSSThe practical winner for many-series + drivers
sktime / DartsUnified forecasting API (classical → DL) + backtestingOSSGood for structured experiments & walk-forward
GluonTS / PyTorch ForecastingDeepAR, N-BEATS, TFT implementationsOSSReach for only with many series + lots of data
TimeGPT (Nixtla)Zero-shot foundation-model forecasts via APIPaidFast baseline / cold-start; validate vs classical
Chronos (Amazon), Moirai (Salesforce)Pre-trained TS foundation modelsOSSZero-shot; promising, not an automatic win
Cloud forecasting (SageMaker, Vertex, Azure ML)Managed training/serving/monitoringPaidConvenient; watch lock-in & cost — see MLOps & Productionization
For deeper production patterns (serving, monitoring, retraining) this table only points the way — that's the MLOps module's territory.

SituationA full forecasting build is overkill — do this instead
Two years of flat monthly dataSeasonal-naïve baseline; a fancy model won't beat "same as last year" by much
You need it this week, little historyFoundation-model zero-shot or seasonal-naïve; validate, then decide if more is worth it
The decision is made twice a year by a personGive them a dashboard with the trend; don't automate a near-zero-volume call
One stable, slow-moving seriesETS/Holt-Winters in an afternoon; stop there
Stakeholder wants an LSTM/Transformer for monthly salesStart classical + a simple ensemble; escalate only on backtested evidence (M-competition)
"We want exact numbers 18 months out"Reset the expectation: long-horizon = wide ranges, not precise points
Intermittent, near-zero demand on most SKUsSpecialised intermittent methods (e.g. Croston) or a simple rule; don't chase MAPE on zeros

The senior move is the same as everywhere in this path: prove the seasonal-naïve baseline isn't already good enough before reaching for anything complex. On forecasting, the simple thing wins far more often than enthusiasm expects.

• Hyndman & Athanasopoulos — Forecasting: Principles and Practice (free online, "FPP3") — the standard, clear, low-maths reference for everything in §1-§7. Read the decomposition, ETS/ARIMA, and evaluation chapters.
  • Aileen Nielsen — Practical Time Series Analysis — pragmatic, code-oriented overview. (Paid)

• The M-competition retrospectives (Makridakis et al., incl. M4/M5) — the evidence behind "simple methods + ensembles win." Skim the conclusions.
  • Nixtla, sktime, and Darts documentation/tutorials — the fastest path to a backtested, many-series forecast.
  • Foundation-model papers/cards (TimeGPT, Chronos, Moirai) — for the 2026 zero-shot landscape. Date everything.

• Deeper math → Time Series Analysis & Forecasting (AI Scientist Path) — the statistics behind stationarity, ARIMA, state-space models, and probabilistic forecasting.
  • Deeper code → Classical ML Mastery (ML Engineer Path) — production-grade feature pipelines, training, and serving for forecasting.
  • Within this path → Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice (the boosted-tree engine) and Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation (the validation discipline).

subsections.title: Decision Checklist — "Is this forecast trustworthy and sellable?"

items.html: Have we run a seasonal-naïve baseline, and does the model beat it (MASE < 1)?
Are we validating with walk-forward / backtesting, never a random split?
Did we look at trend/seasonality (and autocorrelation) before choosing a method?
Is every feature computed from information available at the forecast time (no leakage)?
Are we delivering a range / quantiles, not just a point?
Did we start classical and escalate only on evidence (M-competition lesson)?
Are horizon and retraining cadence agreed and priced in?
Is the forecast wired into the client's planning workflow/tool, not a notebook?

blocks.html:
• A forecast validated with a random train/test split. (It read tomorrow's newspaper; the score is fiction.)
  • No seasonal-naïve baseline. (You can't claim a win you never measured against the trivial rule.)
  • A point forecast sold as certainty. (Reality lands outside it, and trust dies.)
  • MAPE quoted on intermittent / near-zero series. (It explodes; use MASE.)
  • Leaking a future value into a lag/rolling feature. (Backtest looks great, production fails.)
  • Opening with an LSTM/Transformer for a single monthly series. (Boosted trees or ETS first.)
  • Promising precise long-horizon point forecasts. (Accuracy decays; offer ranges.)
  • "Deploy once and we're done." (Series drift — budget monitoring & retraining.)

• "What decision changes once this forecast exists (reorder, staffing, dispatch), and what is being wrong worth today?"
  • "How far ahead, how often, and at what grain do you need it (daily? hourly? per SKU? per store)?"
  • "Can you act on a range, or does a process force one number — and is under- or over-forecasting the costlier mistake?"
  • "How much clean history do we have, and how many related series (one line, or thousands of SKUs)?"
  • "What external drivers move this — price, promotions, weather, holidays — and do we have that data?"
  • "How fast does the world change here, so we can set the retraining cadence?"
  • "Where will the forecast appear so someone actually plans against it?"
Before moving on, you should be able to:

items.html: Explain what makes time series special and why a random train/test split is forbidden
Decompose a series into trend / seasonal / residual and say whether it's additive or multiplicative
Route a forecasting problem across classical → boosted-trees-on-features → deep / foundation models, and justify not defaulting to deep learning
Insist on walk-forward backtesting and a seasonal-naïve baseline, and read MAPE vs sMAPE vs MASE correctly
